speaker: interviewer
text: Edith Abrahams, tape 3, October 18th, 1995. Mrs. Abrahams, we ended the last tape and you were in the restaurant with your sisters and helping your father get established.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. This restaurant was very important, but if-- my father was audacious there, too. He even was catering to some German corporate offices around the corner, which was kind of crazy. But they were willing to get the food from us. And also, we had two waitresses. But the laws came out were-- there was no fraternizing between the Jews and the Germans. And they were working part-time and they liked working for us. They had to go. There was an incident that was extremely disturbing to me. At that point, it was full-scale against Jews. You could not go into restaurants, stores, movies, because wherever you looked, it had signs on, it says 'Juden nicht erwunscht,' which means 'Jews are not wanted' or 'no Jews' or whatever in many different versions. Some of them where it looked like Hebrew letters. This is the way it was. There was no doubt about it. But other things were very disturbing, where the edicts said were-- it was changing daily. The racial laws were in effect. Jews, young kids, could not fraternize, Jews and non-Jews. It was heavily spelled out that the Aryans were pure Aryans down to several generations. And when you're going out on the street, you had loud speakers. They were going on constantly. Goebbels was the one who thought up some of the laws and Goering was the one who was advertising them. And the newspaper that was all over the place was called Der Stuermer. This was the one that had all these doctored up photos and doctored up whatever. And since it was a dictatorial country, there was no other way you'd get any information. The radio had only what they wished to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this one particular day, I was out, and this was in downtown Frankfurt, and they had these placards up. It was on a kiosk. A kiosk is a circular kind of a structure where advertisements were put if someone wanted to rent an apartment or wanted to advertise selling a car or something. And they were plastered with the stories about the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing that was so frightening were the photos were enlarged. These were the photos that came right out that newspaper, right out of Der Stuermer. And some of them were the ones that were babies were killed and the blood was being used for the Seder, our Passover service, and that the Jews drink that. They mix it into their wine. This is something that, for a rational person, could not possibly believe. But there it was. It was plastered all over. And it was so offensive. And the pictures of the Jews were people that I-- no one that I knew looked like that. I mean, horrible looking. They looked like devils, really. And the people were standing and they were laughing. And the thing-- and I'm standing there and I'm thinking to myself, they're talking about me. It's me. It's people like me. And it was very, extremely upsetting because [DRINKS] when you think about it, there are laws that protect animals. And a dog, a bird had the protection of the government. We didn't have any. We were considered lepers. And the terrible thing is if you stand aside and look, and you're not involved, it's different. But when you are the person that you are being ridiculed and laughed at, then you get a feeling that you're a non-person. And the terrible thing was not just the pictures, but the voice of Goering was screaming. And I came home, I was destroyed. I was-- it was awful. It was just awful. I was a teenager. It was a difficult, difficult time for me. And my father luckily recognized it was a crucial time in my life. Papi sat me down and said to me, look, this is not new. This has existed for centuries. Jews have always been a minority, and minorities such as us have suffered. But we will not die, we will prevail. But you must remember, we're the people of the book. We believe in education. We believe in ethics. These are our laws. So long as you hold on to that, you'll be fine. I don't think I ever forgot that. And this is something that became really my backbone for all those years. But it's pretty bad for a kid to grow up to have no identity. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After that-- this was 1936-- things got worse practically daily. We contacted our family in the United States and they finally said to come. And it took us a long time, but we finally got our visas. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their hesitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided-- I didn't really-- they didn't really ever come out and tell me-- but I decided I think the reason they did that is because they were afraid they had to support us. My grandmother's sister and brother, who became our wonderful family when I came here, had visited us in 1928. And my dad, who had a very good position, we were considered wealthy, and they probably looked us over and decided that if we were coming to the United States, they would have to give us this kind of life, which was very foolish, because we were not looking to be supported. I don't think any Jewish refugee looked for that. But that was the reason. I believe that was their reason. And, also, one of the reasons, which was not their fault, the quotas, unfortunately, were extremely small. I fault President Roosevelt for that. The quotas were so small that many, many people would have been saved if they hadn't had that particular-- that these quotas were so little. We finally, in 19-- it went on until-- until we got the papers and everything. My sister, Renata, she left first. She left for the United States. And since she was already-- by that time, she was 23-- she could go by herself. And she had her own passport, she came first. Then, my sister Margot, who had a fiance at that time, she came with her fiance. She left about a couple months before we did. And Papi, Mutti, and I, we went to the embassy in Stuttgart. You had to-- before you came to the United States, you had to visit a doctor. That was very important because the rules, health rules, in the United States are you cannot have an eye disease or you cannot have a lung disease. It was very important. Unfortunately, I caught pneumonia and I was in the hospital in August of 1938. And when I came out of the hospital, of course, there's always a little shadow on your lung. I didn't even think about it until we went to Stuttgart. There was a town in the middle of Germany. And my parents both had gone in to see the doctor. They went in and out in no time. And I came next. And when I went inside, the doctor was an American. I don't-- I wasn't sure-- I'm not sure the nurse was American. She spoke German fluently. But when he examined me, he examined my chest, I thought, uh-oh. He hears with a stethoscope. There's a rattle. And since I didn't speak English, I tried to tell the nurse that I'd had a catarrh. Catarrh is like a bronchial kind of a cough. And I said, but I'm much better now. And because I heard him say something to her and it sounded like Roentgen. Roentgen is x-ray in German. And I thought if he x-rays me, he's going to know that I have a shadow on my lung or something, some discrepancy, and I don't think they're going to let me come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he listened to the nurse and he looked at me and he waved his hand. And I decided, at that point, that a wave of a hand can decide your fate. Because if it hadn't been for that, I would never have come out. I went outside in the hall to my parents and I explained what happened and I said, would you have-- what would you have done if I would not have gotten my visa? They said, we wouldn't have left. We would have had to stay. And so I was very grateful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were getting aboard ship in-- oh, I forgot. There was something I must tell you. I did something extremely stupid before we left. It was a year before, I think it is important you know about that. My parents who had let me do two things. One thing, they had let me go to the north to visit my aunt who had remarried, which I'd lost my uncle, and she had remarried. And they had a pawn shop and she let me stay there. I decided that I had the freedom that I hadn't had when I was a teenager. And I spend a morning going for a walk. And that was in Bremerhaven, that was a harbor city. I took a walk on the dock and I saw the sailor coming towards me. They had these names of a ship across there, and I realized that they got off a ship. You know, German sailors. And it was extremely interesting and I was curious. And I stood in front of this ship and I looked at it. It was a destroyer. And you could see it was damaged. It had damaged. And it looked like it had been in a skirmish. And as the sailors were coming off, one of them stopped me and asked me what I wanted. And I said, oh, I was just curious about this ship. I had disregarded completely that I was Jewish and this is a German sailor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, would you like to see what it looks like inside? And I  said, oh, I'd love to. And he said, well, I'm going to tell them that you're my sister and you want to see my quarters. Would you come? I said, oh, of course. So he took it down into the ship, it was narrow. And I saw that they had certainly been in a skirmish because there were damage, not on the outside, on the inside, and so on. And he took me down, then he took me out again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we sat in front of the ship and he said to me, well, now that I showed you, you have to give me a-- you've got to go out on a date with me. And it hit me, all of a sudden, what I had done. It was stupid. And so he said, what your name. I gave him a name, anything that popped in my head that was a German name. And he asked me where I lived, and I gave him a fictitious address. I didn't know anything about Bremerhaven. And I said I'd meet him the next day for lunch, and he loved that. And then I quickly ran back to my aunt and uncle, and it dawned on me what I had done. I had endangered my whole family, because if that had come out, a Jewish girl with a sailor on a ship that had been-- and that ship had been on a skirmish-- I think it was a skirmish that they had provoked in Spain, the Germans. And I went in to my aunt and uncle's place and they said to me-- you know, asked me-- and I didn't tell him what happened. And the next day, their apartment was right next to the store. And there was like a hallway in between. And my aunt asked me to bring my uncle some coffee. And I had this tray, and I was just going to open the door of the store, and there was a sailor looking into the case.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took one look, and that was the sailor that had taken me onto the ship. And I thought, oh, my god. And I just closed the door real quick and I went back. I must've been white as a sheet, because my aunt said, you look sick. Are you all right? And I said, I feel sick. And I was. I really felt sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never told my parents. I told no one about that incident ever. As a matter of fact, you're the first one I'm telling it to. And it was something that was very foolish. But you do foolish things when you're a kid. The other thing I didn't tell you was that I-- just before we left for about six months, I had an opportunity for two things. Firstly, I joined a dance class that the Germans had let us have, what they thought was a dance class. It was actually a social group of dance that was in that same building where we'd had the restaurant. And in that place, I had found a little happiness, but it lasted very short. It lasted maybe four or five months, then they closed it again. And I, also, there, I met a nice young boy. His name is Arnold Mischel. He had no family, he only had a mother. And we were kids and we thought we were in love. It was very naive and very innocent. But it was the pleasure of a teenager that I enjoyed before we left. And it was important. I tell you about that because of the discovery later about that boy. He told me when he left that, when we were leaving, that he had-- there was no way that he could get out of the country. He had no family anywhere. He only had his mother. I took his address with me and he begged me to try and get him a visa. And that's the last I heard of-- his name was Arnold Mischel. Anyway, we left finally in-- on October 29th, I believe it was. We left Bremerhaven. And when I got aboard the ship, they looked- I had gotten this coat. I called it a teddy bear coat. It was like a fake fur and I loved it. And when I was stepping on board ship, and they said, wait, and they thought that I was hiding money inside there. And they were going to cut the lining. And I cried and I asked them to please leave my coat alone. And they could feel there was nothing in it. And I thought it was indignation to the last. But once we got aboard ship, we felt we left Germany. You really didn't because it was a German line. You couldn't get on any other European line. We were on board a German ship. It was called the Europa. It was a sister ship of the Bremen. And we finally arrived in New York in-- on November 4th, 1930. I never forget, when we passed the Statue of Liberty, we were all pressed together against the railing and everybody cried. We did not stop at Ellis Island at that point, even though they were still stopping at Ellis Island, because a great many Americans were aboard ship and they didn't want to inconvenience them. And then we were being held in a different area, because all the Americans. We were getting off the ship a little later. And since my name was with a W, I knew it would be late. We'd have to wait longer. So I wandered down into our cabin and I found some packages of food that the steward had left for us. And we thought he had been a Nazi. I was really surprised. And I was looking for him to thank him. And, you know, it was something very unexpected. And he gave me the menu because I hadn't taken it. And he was just luck, which I hadn't expected from him at all. So surprises. We then went to the station. And I saw the first time I saw a black person, and I was very frightened. In Germany, I hadn't seen any Negroes. And they asked to-- I was-- I had all the luggage on a pile and I was sitting on top of it. And they asked me, I don't know what they asked. They probably asked could I need help. And all I said, no, no, no, no, no. And they were dancing around and singing, no, no, no, no. And I was completely embarrassed. And I swore that I would learn to speak English and not ever feel like a fool again. And the next-- it took us, we traveled all night and the next day, we came to Cleveland. And two cousins picked us up, and I couldn't understand a word they were saying. And we probably look very disheveled. And I felt like what you call a greenhorn, a typical refugee. And we had a very nice loving family awaiting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who remained back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who remained back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, my Uncle Jack, and his wife and boys. You know, there was my mother's brothers. The one, Wilhelm, the one in [NON-ENGLISH] had died, but his wife had remained, and the two daughters remained. Also, cousins from my grandfather's side. And then, as I said, my mother's two brothers and their families remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, wait a minute. My grandfather also remained. He had remarried, and he was, at that point, in his 70s. And we had no visa for them. And what did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard-- did you hear about the fate of those who remained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When we-- finally, when we settled, we heard about every single one of them. The ones from Uncle Jack, he came in 1939. And he told us that he had been interred immediately after the Kristallnacht. Because this is what happened. They took all the men right after the Kristallnacht. They pulled in the very next day, the 10th of November, pulled in all the men in Frankfurt. And my Uncle Jack was taken into concentration camp. He stayed in Buchenwald and he was there for six months. And he told us that what he had suffered and he had lost a great deal of weight. And he was lucky to get out with the wife and children. And one of the reasons that he was able to get out was that my aunt had dual citizenship. She had partially still a citizenship of Latvia. And because of that, they were able to leave. With my Uncle Saul, it was a different story. He was-- he knew that he was going to be arrested. He was on a trolley. And he told my-- he whispered to my aunt that he was going-- we were going to have a problem. But he was lucky, he got out. They were able to get out. My Aunt Lily, that was a very poignant story because she had no chance to get out. Her two daughter-- her husband had died. My uncle had died. She had remarried a man by the name of Dr. Frankfurter. They had separated, and then divorced. He came to Chicago. And her two daughters had come. One had come directly to the United States, and one, the daughter Ruth, had gone to Holland first. And I think, because of that, things changed for her. She decided to take a chance. We heard all this later. This was not immediately. She had taken a-- she had gone on a ship called the St. Louis, which was leaving Hamburg in 1939. It was supposed to be leaving on the May 13, 1939. But before it left, it was going to Havana, Cuba. There were close to 1,000 people aboard ship. There were 936 passengers, actually. 930 were Jewish. But there was a very corrupt immigration officer in Havana and he had collected the landing certificate from every single person on this ship. He had collected $160, which was a lot of money. And he had pocketed that money. And the officials that worked with him realized that and they put up-- apparently, they let the government know. And so the government just decided that the ship was not going to land, that the people should have visas. But since the people had bought their landing certificates before this new edict in Cuba, apparently, the Hamburg America line, you know, the German ship line, thought it was safe. And so did the people. The passengers thought it was safe. They had no reason not to believe that. And they got aboard ship on the 13th of May, and the ship traveled to Havana. And when they got to Havana, the president of Cuba would not let them get off the ship. Because he said, we said that it would not be-- from the 5th of May on, we told you that these landing certificates would not be eligible. So you had exactly one day. If they would have arrived on the sixth, it would have been all right. After the sixth, they were no good anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here are close to 1,000 people who could not board Havana. And I could just picture my-- I mean, I have no idea. I don't know word for word, but I visualize my aunt on that ship because her daughter had gone-- both daughters were in New York. And since the ship couldn't land, it was traveling in circles around the coast of Florida. It was 90 miles away from the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it just so happened the captain was not a Nazi. He was very unhappy about this. And he wanted to let the passengers land, in either, he didn't care where. But he felt they shouldn't go back to Germany. And the problem was that the whole world sat by and didn't lift a finger, every single country didn't-- they were just ignoring the fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the ship went back, was supposedly to go back to Germany, but luckily the King of Belgium offered to take the refugees. And then the other countries relented. And my aunt, you know, different countries took a small amount. Why she went to Holland, I think it's because my cousin had been there first. I don't know whether she thought they were still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, there was some correspondence with her daughters for a little while. And she must have gotten frightened or something uncomfortable being hidden. She was hidden in Amsterdam. She got on the trolley and she was caught. And the information I have, she was taken to Westerbork, a concentration camp in Amsterdam. And from there, eventually, she was taken to a camp called Sobibor, which was not a work camp. It was a killing factory. And she perished in Sobibor. I have the date. I can't remember exactly, but she died there. The rest of the family, my cousin, who was a very favorite cousin of mine, had gone to Australia. He had gone, had been interred in a camp in southern-- in-- excuse me, southern France. And then he immigrated to Australia. He literally he tossed a coin. It was came up. It was either Africa or Australia. He decided on Australia. And the rest of the family all came out. Some went to Argentina, the rest all came to New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were all still close together. But I myself worked in a bakery in Cleveland. And I worked in a Jewish bakery. I worked with this very lovely family there. And, then, I applied-- I decided-- it was a small bakery. And I decided I wanted a different more responsible job, because I was just a sales girl. I knew that there was a job available. There was a-- since baking, bakery was my line at that point, I know there was a chain. It was called the Kaiser Company, you know, like a bakery chain. But I had heard that they didn't hire Jews. And I decided-- I was surprised that this was happening in the United States. There was anti-Semitism here too. But I decided-- a foolish thing. I decided to make an application. And they had religion on there. And I wrote Jewish. And underneath, in parentheses, I wrote, 'I'm aware of your policy.' And then I left and went home. And when I got home, there was a phone call already and they said they wanted to see me and I was hired the next day. And I decided that maybe I was a token for the-- because of their policy, they wanted to hire me not to have that image. But I didn't care because I felt I started something for other Jewish girls. And I became a manager for them for two particular bakeries. And from there, I went to another kind of a vocation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was--
